_meta.authoringNotes: Diagnostic and management framework anchored to the 2006 Brain Trauma Foundation/AANS/CNS guidelines on surgical management of acute epidural hematomas (Bullock et al. Neurosurgery 2006, PMID 16540742) and updated 2017 Severe TBI Guidelines (Carney et al. Neurosurgery, PMID 27654000). Spinal epidural hematoma management per Liao et al. Spine 2008. Pediatric considerations per Schutzman & Greenes Pediatrics 2001. Epidemiology and outcomes per Bricolo & Pasut J Neurosurg 1984, Lee et al. J Trauma 1998 (PMID 9529189), and Gerlach et al. Childs Nerv Syst 2009. Anticoagulant reversal per Frontera et al. Neurocritical Care 2016 (PMID 26714677).

h1Title: Epidural Hematoma: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: An epidural hematoma (also called extradural hematoma) is a collection of blood between the skull and the dura mater, most often caused by tearing of the middle meningeal artery in association with a temporal bone fracture. It is a true neurosurgical emergency because the high-pressure arterial bleeding can expand rapidly and produce fatal brain compression within hours. The classic clinical pattern includes a brief loss of consciousness from the initial head injury, a temporary 'lucid interval' lasting minutes to hours during which the patient appears recovered, and then rapid deterioration with severe headache, vomiting, decreased consciousness, hemiparesis, and unilateral pupil dilation. Epidural hematoma accounts for roughly 2% of all head injury admissions and 5-15% of severe traumatic brain injuries. With prompt diagnosis on CT (the characteristic biconvex 'lens-shaped' hyperdensity that does not cross suture lines) and rapid surgical evacuation, mortality has dropped from 40-50% historically to 5-15% in modern series, with good functional outcomes in 70-80% of patients reaching surgery before fixed pupil dilation. Spinal epidural hematoma is a rarer but analogous condition causing acute spinal cord compression.

keyStats.prevalence: Epidural hematoma occurs in approximately 2% of head injury admissions and 5-15% of severe traumatic brain injuries (Bullock 2006, contemporary trauma registries)
keyStats.demographics: Men affected 3-4x more often than women; peak incidence ages 15-40, predominantly from motor vehicle crashes, falls, and assault; rare in adults over 60 because of dural adhesion with age
keyStats.avgAge: Median age 20-30 years; rare in infants under 2 (dural adhesion to skull) and in adults over 60
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 100,000+ cases worldwide annually; substantial disability and mortality, particularly in regions with limited access to emergency neurosurgical care (GBD 2021)

definition: An epidural hematoma (ICD-10: S06.4 traumatic epidural hemorrhage; I62.1 nontraumatic extradural hemorrhage; sometimes called extradural hematoma) is a collection of blood between the inner table of the skull and the outer (periosteal) layer of the dura mater. The most common cause is tearing of the middle meningeal artery as the temporal bone fractures across its course; less commonly the middle meningeal vein, dural venous sinus, or diploic vein is the source. Bleeding accumulates rapidly because arterial pressure separates the dura from the inner table, producing a focal collection limited by the firm adherence of the dura to skull sutures — hence the classic biconvex (lens-shaped) appearance that does not cross suture lines on CT. Most epidural hematomas occur in the temporal or temporo-parietal region above the middle meningeal artery, but they can occur at the vertex, posterior fossa, frontal region, and along the spine (spinal epidural hematoma). Approximately 20% of cranial epidural hematomas occur without skull fracture, particularly in younger patients with more pliable skulls. Spinal epidural hematomas occur most commonly in the thoracic and cervical spine, often without obvious trauma and frequently in patients on anticoagulation. Neurosurgical evaluation and intervention are time-critical: outcomes depend heavily on speed of diagnosis and surgical evacuation. Management is led by neurosurgery, supported by neurocritical care, neurology, anesthesia, and trauma services in dedicated trauma and neurosciences centers per Brain Trauma Foundation guidelines.

typesClassification.description: The most common type (70-80% of cranial EDH). Caused by middle meningeal artery laceration from temporal bone fracture. Classic biconvex high-density appearance on CT not crossing skull sutures. Rapid expansion possible; surgical urgency depends on size, midline shift, and neurological status.

typesClassification.type: Cranial epidural hematoma — frontal, parietal, vertex, posterior fossa
typesClassification.description: Less common locations with variable mechanisms. Vertex EDH from superior sagittal sinus injury can be subtle on routine CT. Posterior fossa EDH is potentially catastrophic because small volumes compress the brainstem; surgical threshold is lower than supratentorial.

Blood collection in the spinal epidural space, most commonly cervical or thoracic. Causes acute back pain followed by radicular symptoms, motor weakness, sensory loss, and bladder/bowel dysfunction. Often spontaneous or associated with anticoagulation, less commonly with trauma or interventional spine procedures. Surgical decompression within 12 hours is associated with better neurological recovery.

Less common — represents slowly expanding venous EDH from middle meningeal vein, diploic vein, or dural sinus injury. May present days to weeks after trauma with progressive headache and focal deficit. Imaging shows mixed-density or hypodense biconvex collection. Surgical management similar to acute EDH.

Children — especially those over 2 years — develop EDH after relatively minor head injury because of less skull-dural adhesion. Often venous source. Lucid interval may be absent. Surgical evacuation typically performed with mortality under 5% in modern pediatric series.

primarySymptoms: Brief initial loss of consciousness from the head injury, lasting seconds to minutes.
A 'lucid interval' lasting minutes to several hours during which the patient appears awake, conversational, and apparently recovered — present in approximately 20-50% of cases.
Severe progressive headache, often unilateral on the side of the bleed, developing during the lucid interval.
Nausea and persistent vomiting, often projectile, as intracranial pressure rises.
Rapidly decreasing level of consciousness from drowsiness to stupor and coma, sometimes over 30-90 minutes.
Hemiparesis (weakness on one side of the body), most commonly affecting the limbs contralateral to the hematoma.
Unilateral pupil dilation on the side of the hematoma (uncal herniation compressing the third cranial nerve) — an ominous late sign requiring immediate surgery.
Seizures, occurring in 5-15% of patients during the acute phase.
Cushing's triad of hypertension, bradycardia, and irregular respiration — very late signs of brainstem compression.
For spinal epidural hematoma: sudden severe back pain followed by progressive motor weakness, sensory level, and loss of bladder or bowel function over minutes to hours.
earlyWarningSigns: Brief loss of consciousness with subsequent severe headache after temporal-area head injury
Persistent severe vomiting following head injury
Drowsiness developing minutes to hours after apparent recovery from head injury (lucid interval)
Any new focal neurological deficit (weakness, speech change, vision change) after head injury
Sudden severe back pain followed by progressive lower limb weakness or numbness — possible spinal epidural hematoma; emergency MRI
emergencySigns: Decreasing level of consciousness after head injury — call emergency services and arrange immediate CT
Unilateral pupil dilation with hemiparesis — uncal herniation, requires emergency surgical evacuation; mortality rises sharply without immediate intervention
Hypertension, bradycardia, and irregular respiration (Cushing's triad) — late sign of raised intracranial pressure
Sudden severe back pain with motor weakness, sensory level, or bladder/bowel dysfunction — spinal epidural hematoma requiring emergency neurosurgical decompression
Cardiorespiratory arrest following head injury — initiate ALS and arrange emergency CT once stable

causes.cause: Middle meningeal artery laceration from temporal bone fracture
causes.description: The most common mechanism (50-75% of cranial EDH). Force applied to the temporal region fractures the relatively thin squamous portion of the temporal bone across the course of the middle meningeal artery, lacerating the vessel. Arterial bleeding produces rapidly expanding biconvex collection. Most common with falls, assault, motor vehicle crashes, and sports injuries.

causes.cause: Venous injury (middle meningeal vein, dural venous sinus, diploic vein)
causes.description: Accounts for 20-25% of cranial EDH. Slower expansion than arterial EDH, sometimes producing a more delayed clinical presentation. Vertex EDH from superior sagittal sinus injury and posterior fossa EDH from transverse or sigmoid sinus injury fall into this category.

causes.cause: Head injury without skull fracture in younger patients
causes.description: Approximately 20% of cranial EDH occurs without identifiable skull fracture, particularly in younger patients whose more pliable skulls can flex without breaking. The bleeding still arises from temporary separation of dura from skull during impact, with subsequent vessel injury.

Warfarin, direct oral anticoagulants, antiplatelets, hemophilia, thrombocytopenia, and liver disease increase EDH formation risk and accelerate expansion after any trauma. Patients on anticoagulation have lower threshold for both initial bleeding and surgical evacuation.

causes.cause: Spinal interventions and anticoagulation (for spinal EDH)
causes.description: Spinal epidural hematoma may follow lumbar puncture, epidural anesthesia, spinal block, or spinal surgery, particularly in anticoagulated patients. Spontaneous spinal EDH also occurs in anticoagulated patients without an identified procedure or trauma. Vascular malformations are a rare cause.

Children and adolescents are at risk for EDH from minor head injury because of less dural-skull adhesion. Common mechanisms: falls from playground equipment, sports impact, bicycle accidents. Helmet use prevents many pediatric EDH cases.

riskFactors.description: Men have 3-4x higher incidence than women, predominantly explained by higher rates of trauma exposure (motor vehicle crashes, sports, assault, alcohol-related falls).
Peak EDH incidence in young adulthood when high-energy trauma exposure is highest. Less common after age 60 because the dura becomes more firmly adhered to skull.

riskFactors.factor: Temporal head impact and skull fracture
riskFactors.description: Direct blunt force to the temple is the most common mechanism. Skull fracture is present in 75-80% of cranial EDH. Helmets, seatbelts, and other protective measures reduce risk.
Warfarin, DOACs, and antiplatelets accelerate EDH expansion and worsen outcomes after any head trauma. Modern reversal protocols reduce but do not eliminate the impact.
Alcohol-related falls and assaults are a major mechanism. Alcohol may also impair coagulation and reduce protective reflexes during impact.

riskFactors.factor: Sports and recreational activities without protection
riskFactors.description: Cycling, motorcycling, contact sports, skiing, and skateboarding without proper helmet use carry elevated EDH risk. Mandatory helmet laws and protective gear reduce incidence.

riskFactors.factor: Pediatric age (over 2 years) with playground or bicycle injury
riskFactors.description: Children over 2 have less dural adhesion to skull and develop EDH from relatively minor head impacts. Helmet use during cycling and skating prevents many cases.

affectedDemographics: Young adult men: predominant demographic, mostly from motor vehicle crashes, assault, falls, and sports injuries
Children over 2 years: vulnerable to EDH from minor head injury; helmet use is protective
Patients on anticoagulation: higher risk of bleeding from any trauma and worse outcomes without rapid reversal
Areas with limited access to emergency neurosurgical care: higher mortality due to delayed diagnosis and intervention
Spinal EDH patients: often older adults on anticoagulation; some without identifiable trauma
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of cranial epidural hematoma is established by emergency non-contrast CT of the head, the gold-standard imaging in any patient with significant head injury or new neurological symptoms. EDH appears as a biconvex (lens-shaped) hyperdense collection between skull and brain that does not cross skull suture lines (limited by dural adherence at sutures). Associated findings include skull fracture in 75-80% of cases, mass effect with midline shift, basal cistern compression, and sometimes a 'swirl sign' (low-density area within the hyperdense hematoma indicating active bleeding and rapid expansion). MRI is more sensitive in subacute cases or when CT is equivocal but is rarely used in the emergency setting because of time and access. Spinal epidural hematoma is best diagnosed by urgent MRI of the symptomatic spine region; CT may miss it. Clinical assessment includes Glasgow Coma Scale, pupil examination, motor function, brainstem reflexes, mechanism of injury, time course (especially documenting lucid interval), and review of medications. Coagulation studies (PT/INR, aPTT, platelet count, fibrinogen) and DOAC levels are obtained urgently. Marshall and Rotterdam CT classifications grade severity and predict outcomes. Repeat CT is indicated if neurological status changes or to confirm stability in observed patients. Cervical spine and other systemic injuries are evaluated in parallel in trauma patients. Time from arrival to definitive imaging and to surgical intervention is the most important modifiable factor in outcomes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line and gold-standard imaging for EDH; identifies biconvex hyperdense collection, skull fracture, mass effect, and other intracranial injuries
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Rapid CT scan, 5-10 minutes in the scanner. Any patient with significant head injury, decreased consciousness, focal deficit, persistent vomiting, or seizure receives immediate CT. Findings communicated to neurosurgery on call urgently.

diagnosticTests.test: Repeat CT head at 6-12 hours or with deterioration
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects hematoma expansion in observed patients; standard in conservatively managed EDH or post-surgical surveillance
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Repeat scan when clinically indicated. Expansion of small EDH triggers conversion to surgical management. Standard at 24-48 hours after surgery to confirm complete evacuation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: More sensitive than CT for very small or atypical collections, posterior fossa EDH, and underlying vascular malformations
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes in the scanner. Used selectively when CT is equivocal or for atypical presentations. Rarely first-line in acute trauma.

diagnosticTests.test: Urgent spine MRI for suspected spinal epidural hematoma
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies spinal epidural hematoma with site, extent, and degree of cord compression
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-60 minute MRI of the suspected spine level. Critical for diagnosis — sensitivity far higher than CT. Emergency-eligible patients receive priority slots.

diagnosticTests.test: Glasgow Coma Scale, pupillary examination, and motor exam
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies neurological severity and tracks deterioration; baseline for surgical decision-making
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard bedside assessment performed at presentation and repeatedly. Drop of 2 or more points on GCS or new pupillary asymmetry triggers re-imaging and likely surgery.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies coagulopathy contributing to bleed and guides reversal
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Urgent blood sample: PT/INR, aPTT, platelet count, fibrinogen; DOAC levels or anti-Xa activity if relevant; thromboelastography (TEG/ROTEM) at major trauma centers.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects other significant injuries (cervical spine, intra-abdominal, thoracic) that affect management and prognosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Pan-CT (head, cervical spine, chest, abdomen, pelvis) is standard in major trauma. FAST ultrasound for abdominal hemorrhage. Trauma team coordination with neurosurgery for prioritization.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is surgical evacuation for the great majority of clinically significant epidural hematomas. Brain Trauma Foundation guidelines indicate surgery for: hematoma volume >30 mL regardless of GCS; or GCS <9 with anisocoria; or any neurological deterioration during observation; or significant midline shift or mass effect. Standard procedure is craniotomy (open skull operation) with evacuation of the clot, identification and control of the bleeding vessel (typically the middle meningeal artery), and dural tack-up to the skull edges to prevent re-accumulation. Decompressive craniectomy is added when brain swelling is severe. Hospital stay is typically 5-14 days for uncomplicated EDH; longer in severe TBI. Observation with serial CT and neurological monitoring in a neurocritical care setting is appropriate for small (<30 mL), thin (<15 mm), with minimal mass effect (<5 mm midline shift), neurologically stable EDH in patients with GCS ≥14. Anticoagulation reversal is urgent: prothrombin complex concentrate plus vitamin K for warfarin; andexanet alfa for factor Xa inhibitors; idarucizumab for dabigatran; platelet transfusion when antiplatelets are involved. Blood pressure control (systolic 140-160 mmHg pre-surgery; 100-140 mmHg post-surgery), antiepileptic prophylaxis with levetiracetam for 7-14 days, ICP management with head elevation, hyperosmolar therapy, and CSF drainage where needed, and aggressive systemic resuscitation form the medical adjuncts. Spinal epidural hematoma requires emergency surgical decompression — laminectomy with evacuation — ideally within 12 hours of symptom onset for best neurological recovery. Rehabilitation begins in the acute hospital and continues as inpatient or outpatient depending on residual deficit. The time from injury to definitive surgical intervention is the strongest modifiable factor in survival and neurological outcome — every hour of delay raises mortality and disability.

medicalTreatments.name: Urgent anticoagulation and antiplatelet reversal
medicalTreatments.description: Warfarin: 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate 25-50 IU/kg plus vitamin K 10 mg IV. Apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban: andexanet alfa or 4-factor PCC. Dabigatran: idarucizumab 5 g IV. Antiplatelets: platelet transfusion considered in active bleeding (evidence base limited). Reversal completed before or during transfer to surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reverses coagulopathy within minutes to hours; reduces hematoma expansion and improves surgical outcomes

medicalTreatments.name: Antiepileptic prophylaxis (levetiracetam 500-1500 mg twice daily for 7-14 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Used for 7-14 days after surgical evacuation per Brain Trauma Foundation guidance to prevent early post-traumatic seizures. Levetiracetam preferred over phenytoin in most cases due to fewer drug interactions and similar efficacy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces early post-traumatic seizures by approximately 60% in randomized trials

medicalTreatments.name: Hyperosmolar therapy for raised intracranial pressure (mannitol or hypertonic saline)
medicalTreatments.description: Mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg IV bolus or 3% hypertonic saline 250 mL IV bolus to lower ICP before or after surgical decompression. Used as bridge to definitive surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Lowers ICP within 15-30 minutes; bridge to surgical evacuation

medicalTreatments.name: Blood pressure management protocols
medicalTreatments.description: Pre-surgery systolic target 140-160 mmHg with labetalol, nicardipine, clevidipine, or hydralazine. Post-surgery individualized but typically lower targets to limit edema while maintaining cerebral perfusion. Avoidance of hypotension <90 mmHg systolic.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces hematoma expansion and secondary brain injury

medicalTreatments.name: Conservative observation for small asymptomatic EDH (selected cases)
medicalTreatments.description: Hematomas <30 mL volume, <15 mm thickness, <5 mm midline shift, in patients with GCS ≥14 and stable neurological exam, can be observed in a neurocritical care setting with serial CT at 6, 12, and 24 hours. Conversion to surgery for any expansion or deterioration.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Avoids surgery in approximately 30% of small EDH; close monitoring essential because of risk of sudden expansion

medicalTreatments.name: Comprehensive ICP and CPP management for severe TBI
medicalTreatments.description: ICP target <22 mmHg, cerebral perfusion pressure 60-70 mmHg per Brain Trauma Foundation guideline. Includes head elevation, sedation, hyperosmolar therapy, mild hyperventilation as rescue, CSF drainage via external ventricular drain, and decompressive craniectomy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Tiered ICP management improves outcomes in severe TBI; CRASH-3 trial showed tranexamic acid benefit when given within 3 hours in moderate TBI

surgicalOptions.name: Craniotomy with epidural hematoma evacuation
surgicalOptions.description: Standard operation for symptomatic or large EDH. Bone flap raised over the hematoma; clot evacuated; bleeding vessel identified and controlled (usually middle meningeal artery coagulation or wax); dural tack-up sutures to skull edge prevent re-accumulation. Bone flap replaced and secured.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Mortality 5-15% in modern series with prompt surgery; good functional outcome (mRS 0-2) in 70-80% of patients reaching surgery before fixed pupil dilation

surgicalOptions.name: Burr hole evacuation (selected cases)
surgicalOptions.description: Used in select cases — small accessible EDH, deteriorating patients before transfer to definitive care, or remote settings. Less complete evacuation than craniotomy; often converted to formal craniotomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Acceptable as temporizing measure; lower complete-evacuation rate than craniotomy

surgicalOptions.name: Decompressive craniectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Performed when severe brain swelling complicates EDH evacuation. Large bone flap removed (not replaced immediately) and dura widely opened. Skull replaced with cranioplasty (autologous bone or custom implant) at 3-6 months when swelling resolves.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reduces mortality in refractory raised ICP after TBI; long-term functional outcomes variable

surgicalOptions.name: Spinal laminectomy with epidural hematoma evacuation
surgicalOptions.description: Emergency operation for spinal epidural hematoma. Laminectomy at affected levels with clot evacuation and decompression of the spinal cord. Time-critical: surgery within 12 hours of symptom onset is associated with substantially better neurological recovery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 70-80% achieve significant neurological improvement if operated within 12 hours; much lower with delayed surgery

alternativeTreatments.name: Conservative non-surgical management of small EDH
alternativeTreatments.description: Carefully selected patients (small volume, no mass effect, intact neurology, GCS ≥14) can be managed with close neurocritical observation and serial imaging. Approximately 30% of EDH can avoid surgery with this approach, but the threshold for intervention is low — any expansion or neurological change triggers surgery.

alternativeTreatments.name: Endovascular embolization of middle meningeal artery (selected cases)
alternativeTreatments.description: Emerging adjunct for selected EDH cases, particularly in patients with bleeding diathesis or as a temporizing measure when surgery is delayed. Not yet standard of care for typical acute EDH but increasingly studied alongside use in chronic subdural hematoma.

whySeeSpecialist: Epidural hematoma is a true neurosurgical emergency requiring 24/7 neurosurgical and trauma center capability. Outcome depends heavily on time from injury to definitive surgical evacuation. Comprehensive trauma centers and dedicated neurotrauma services achieve substantially better outcomes than facilities without immediate neurosurgical coverage. Field triage protocols and helicopter retrieval are designed to minimize time to definitive care.
doctorSelectionGuide: In emergencies, the priority is the nearest hospital with 24/7 neurosurgery and CT capability — not specific surgeon selection. For planned follow-up care or for non-emergent surgical decisions, choose ACS-verified Level I or II trauma centers, neurotrauma programs, and centers with established Brain Trauma Foundation protocol adherence. Confirm 24/7 availability of neurocritical care, interventional neuroradiology, and rehabilitation services.
hospitalCriteria: 24/7 neurosurgical coverage with rapid in-house response
ACS-verified Level I or II trauma center for major trauma EDH
Dedicated neurocritical care unit with neurointensivist coverage
Rapid anticoagulation reversal protocols and pharmacy stocking of reversal agents
Immediate access to CT scanning and 24/7 emergency operating room
Integrated rehabilitation services for inpatient and outpatient recovery
keyFacilities: ACS-verified Level I and II trauma centers
Academic medical centers with neurotrauma programs
Centers participating in the TBI Model Systems research program (US)
Pediatric trauma centers for pediatric EDH
preventionStrategies: Use seatbelts, child car seats, and motorcycle/bicycle helmets per evidence-based guidelines — these prevent the majority of severe head injuries causing EDH.
Enforce graduated driver licensing, impaired driving laws, and speed limits — established traffic safety measures.
Wear sports-specific helmets for skiing, snowboarding, skateboarding, contact sports, and equestrian activity.
Use protective equipment in occupational settings (construction, mining) per workplace safety standards.
Address alcohol use disorder and impaired driving — major contributors to head injury epidemiology.
Educate parents and caregivers on home and playground safety; supervise young children near elevated surfaces.
For patients on anticoagulation, review indication regularly, optimize fall prevention, and consider alternatives such as DOACs or left atrial appendage closure where appropriate.
lifestyleModifications: Follow post-operative restrictions strictly: avoid contact sports, heavy lifting, and Valsalva maneuvers for 6-12 weeks after craniotomy.
Resume activities gradually with neurosurgery clearance; driving typically not permitted for 1-3 months minimum, longer with persistent deficits.
Use prescribed antiepileptic medication for the full prophylaxis duration; discuss long-term need with neurology if seizures occurred.
Attend rehabilitation appointments (physical, occupational, speech, neuropsychological therapy) per the discharge plan.
Manage post-concussive symptoms (headache, fatigue, cognitive slowing, mood changes) — common after TBI and treatable.
Limit alcohol and avoid recreational drugs during recovery; both interact with antiepileptic medications and impair cognitive recovery.
Maintain regular follow-up CT and neurosurgical clinic visits as scheduled.
Wear a medical alert identifier indicating recent neurosurgery if relevant.

recommended: Balanced diet supporting recovery — adequate protein (1.0-1.5 g/kg/day), vitamins, and minerals
Hydration sufficient to maintain normal urine output, especially during ICU phase with sodium and osmolality management
Mediterranean-style eating long term for cardiovascular and brain health
Soft or modified-texture diet if dysphagia is present after severe TBI; speech-language pathology assessment
avoid: Alcohol during recovery — interacts with antiepileptic medications, worsens sleep, and slows cognitive recovery
High-sodium foods in patients with persistent hypertension or SIADH
Heavy meals immediately before lying flat (aspiration risk during early recovery)

exerciseGuidelines: Avoid contact sports, heavy lifting, and Valsalva maneuvers for 6-12 weeks after craniotomy. Begin gentle walking within days to weeks of stability, progressing as tolerated under physiotherapy guidance. Resume non-contact aerobic activity (stationary cycling, walking, swimming) at 6-8 weeks if cleared. Full return to high-impact and contact sports typically requires 3-6 months and individual neurosurgical clearance. For pediatric patients, the same staged return is followed, often slightly slower. Discuss return to driving, work, and sports with the treating neurosurgeon at each follow-up.
dailyManagement: Take antiepileptic medication consistently for the prescribed duration.
Monitor and report any new severe headache, vomiting, decreased consciousness, weakness, vision change, or seizure — could indicate complication or recurrence.
Manage post-concussive symptoms (headache, sleep disturbance, fatigue, mood changes, attention difficulty) through structured rehabilitation.
Use any prescribed mobility aids and follow safe-transfer techniques learned during inpatient rehabilitation.
Maintain regular sleep schedule and avoid significant sleep deprivation, which slows recovery and raises seizure risk.
Attend all scheduled follow-up imaging and clinic appointments.
Engage with social and vocational rehabilitation; return-to-work decisions are individualized and may involve graded duties.
prognosis: Modern outcomes for acute epidural hematoma have improved substantially. With prompt diagnosis and surgical evacuation, 30-day mortality is 5-15%, and 70-80% of patients reaching surgery before fixed pupil dilation achieve good functional outcomes (modified Rankin Scale 0-2). Time from injury to surgical evacuation is the most important modifiable factor: outcomes are best when surgery occurs within 4 hours of injury. Patients arriving with bilateral fixed dilated pupils and decerebrate posturing have mortality approaching 50% even with surgery. Children typically have better outcomes than adults of similar severity due to greater neural plasticity. Persistent neurological deficits (hemiparesis, language deficit, cognitive change) are common in patients with associated severe TBI but recover substantially over months. Post-concussive symptoms (headache, fatigue, mood changes) affect 30-50% of survivors and respond to multidisciplinary post-TBI care. Long-term seizure risk after EDH is 5-15%. Spinal epidural hematoma neurological recovery depends on time to surgery: 70-80% improve significantly with surgery within 12 hours; recovery rates drop substantially with delay. Repeat EDH is rare and usually reflects ongoing coagulopathy or another injury rather than the original EDH. Overall, EDH is one of the most surgically treatable severe traumatic brain injuries when caught and operated promptly.
recoveryTimeline: Acute hospital stay 5-14 days for uncomplicated EDH; longer in severe TBI. Initial recovery of consciousness and focal deficits typically begins within hours to days of surgical evacuation. Rehabilitation in inpatient or outpatient settings over weeks to months; cognitive recovery may continue up to 1-2 years. Return to work, school, and driving is individualized and typically allowed at 1-3 months for uncomplicated cases. Cranioplasty (if craniectomy was performed) at 3-6 months. Long-term seizure prophylaxis is uncommon unless seizures have occurred.
complications: Death from delayed evacuation — mortality rises sharply with each hour from neurological deterioration to surgery
Persistent neurological deficits including hemiparesis, language dysfunction, cognitive impairment, and visual field defects
Post-traumatic epilepsy in 5-15% of survivors, usually within 1-2 years of injury
Recurrent or new intracranial hemorrhage from underlying coagulopathy or repeat trauma
Surgical complications: infection (1-3%), bleeding into surgical site, CSF leak, deep vein thrombosis
Post-concussive syndrome: headache, fatigue, sleep disturbance, mood and attention changes affecting 30-50% of survivors
Loss of independence and substantial disability in patients with severe initial injury or delayed surgery

supportResources.name: Brain Trauma Foundation
supportResources.description: US non-profit providing evidence-based clinical guidelines for traumatic brain injury including EDH, plus public education materials.

supportResources.name: American Association of Neurological Surgeons (AANS) — patient information
supportResources.description: Authoritative patient-facing information from the US neurosurgery professional society.

supportResources.name: Brain Injury Association of America (BIAA)
supportResources.description: US patient advocacy organization providing information, support groups, and resources for brain injury survivors.

supportResources.name: Headway — the brain injury association (UK)
supportResources.description: UK charity providing information, support, and rehabilitation resources for brain injury survivors.

confusedWithConditions.name: Subdural Hematoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Subdural hematoma is beneath the dura, usually from venous bridging vein tearing, with a crescent shape on CT that crosses skull suture lines. Epidural hematoma is between skull and dura, usually from middle meningeal arterial tearing, with a biconvex shape that does not cross sutures. EDH expands faster and is generally more emergent than acute SDH.

confusedWithConditions.name: Stroke
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Stroke includes ischemic infarction and intracerebral hemorrhage. Both can produce focal deficits but originate from intra-axial pathology. EDH is extra-axial and trauma-related. CT distinguishes them: EDH is a biconvex hyperdensity outside the brain; intracerebral hemorrhage is within brain tissue; acute infarct initially has no blood.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cerebral Aneurysm (Subarachnoid Hemorrhage)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage produces thunderclap headache and blood within the subarachnoid space around the circle of Willis. EDH produces post-traumatic deterioration with biconvex extra-axial collection. Both are emergencies but have entirely different treatment pathways.

confusedWithConditions.name: Migraine
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Migraine produces recurrent headaches with characteristic features. EDH produces acute post-traumatic headache with progressive neurological symptoms. The crucial distinction is the history of trauma and the time course; any new severe headache after head injury warrants imaging.

relatedConditions.name: Subdural Hematoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Other major intracranial extra-axial hemorrhage; commonly considered in differential and may coexist with EDH.

relatedConditions.name: Stroke
relatedConditions.relevance: EDH is a form of traumatic intracranial bleeding within the broader stroke and intracranial hemorrhage spectrum.

relatedConditions.name: Cerebral Aneurysm
relatedConditions.relevance: Other major cause of acute intracranial hemorrhage; differential when trauma history is unclear.

relatedConditions.name: Back Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Acute severe back pain with neurological symptoms can indicate spinal epidural hematoma, a related condition.

relatedConditions.name: Atrial Fibrillation
relatedConditions.relevance: Common reason for anticoagulation that complicates and worsens EDH outcomes; requires careful resumption decisions.

faqs.question: What is an epidural hematoma?
faqs.answer: An epidural hematoma is a collection of blood between the skull and the dura mater of the brain, most often caused by tearing of the middle meningeal artery after temporal bone fracture. It is a true neurosurgical emergency because the high-pressure bleed can expand rapidly and cause fatal brain compression within hours.

faqs.question: What is the difference between epidural and subdural hematoma?
faqs.answer: Epidural hematoma is between skull and dura, usually from arterial bleeding (middle meningeal artery), with biconvex shape on CT that does not cross suture lines. Subdural hematoma is beneath the dura, usually from venous bridging veins, with crescent shape crossing suture lines. EDH expands faster and is generally more emergent than acute SDH.

faqs.question: What is a lucid interval?
faqs.answer: A lucid interval is a period of apparent recovery after the initial head injury, lasting minutes to a few hours, during which the patient seems awake and conversational before deteriorating from expanding epidural hematoma. It occurs in 20-50% of EDH cases and is a classic but not universal feature.

faqs.question: How is epidural hematoma diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Non-contrast CT of the head is the gold standard. EDH appears as a biconvex (lens-shaped) hyperdense collection between skull and brain that does not cross skull suture lines. Skull fracture is found in 75-80% of cases. MRI is rarely used in the emergency setting but is more sensitive for atypical or small collections.

faqs.question: How is epidural hematoma treated?
faqs.answer: Most clinically significant EDH require urgent surgical evacuation by craniotomy. The bone flap is raised, clot evacuated, bleeding artery controlled, and dura tacked up. Small asymptomatic EDH can sometimes be observed with serial CT and neurological monitoring. Anticoagulation is reversed urgently when present.

faqs.question: What is the survival rate for epidural hematoma?
faqs.answer: With prompt diagnosis and surgical evacuation, modern 30-day mortality is 5-15%, and 70-80% of patients reaching surgery before fixed pupil dilation achieve good functional outcomes. Outcomes are dramatically worse with delayed treatment, bilateral fixed pupils, or extreme age.

faqs.question: Can an epidural hematoma heal without surgery?
faqs.answer: Small asymptomatic EDH (under 30 mL, less than 15 mm thick, minimal midline shift) in neurologically intact patients can sometimes be managed with close observation and serial CT. About 30% of small EDH avoid surgery this way. The threshold for surgery is low — any expansion or deterioration triggers operation.

faqs.question: What causes most epidural hematomas?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is tearing of the middle meningeal artery from a temporal bone fracture during head impact — motor vehicle crashes, falls, assaults, and sports injuries. Less commonly venous bleeding from dural sinuses or diploic veins. Anticoagulation increases the risk and worsens expansion.

faqs.question: How quickly does an epidural hematoma develop?
faqs.answer: Arterial epidural hematomas can expand rapidly — significant mass effect can develop within 30-90 minutes of injury. Venous EDH expands more slowly, sometimes over hours to days. The lucid interval characteristic of EDH reflects this rapid arterial expansion after initial recovery from concussion.

faqs.question: Can children get epidural hematomas?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children over 2 years are particularly vulnerable to EDH after minor head injury because of less dural-skull adhesion. Common causes include playground falls, bicycle accidents, and sports injuries. Pediatric EDH has lower mortality than adult EDH (under 5% in modern series) and is often venous in origin.

faqs.question: What is a spinal epidural hematoma?
faqs.answer: Spinal epidural hematoma is blood collection in the spinal epidural space, most commonly in the cervical or thoracic spine. It causes sudden severe back pain followed by progressive motor weakness, sensory level, and bladder/bowel dysfunction. Emergency MRI confirms diagnosis; surgical decompression within 12 hours is associated with much better recovery.

faqs.question: What is the lens-shaped or biconvex appearance on CT?
faqs.answer: Epidural hematoma appears as a biconvex (convex on both sides, like a lens) hyperdense collection on CT because the bleeding is contained by firm dural adherence at skull sutures. This shape distinguishes EDH from subdural hematoma, which forms a crescent. Most EDH occur in the temporal region and do not cross suture lines.

faqs.question: How long does it take to recover from epidural hematoma?
faqs.answer: Hospital stay is typically 5-14 days for uncomplicated EDH. Recovery of consciousness and focal deficits begins within hours to days of surgery. Rehabilitation over weeks to months; cognitive recovery may continue up to 1-2 years. Return to work, school, and driving typically allowed at 1-3 months for uncomplicated cases.

faqs.question: Can epidural hematoma cause seizures?
faqs.answer: Yes. Seizures occur in 5-15% of EDH patients, most often during the acute phase or within the first year. Antiepileptic prophylaxis (levetiracetam) for 7-14 days after evacuation reduces early post-traumatic seizures. Long-term antiepileptic therapy is used only for patients who have actually had seizures.

faqs.question: Should I avoid blood thinners after epidural hematoma?
faqs.answer: Anticoagulation is paused acutely. Resumption is individualized by neurosurgery, hematology, and the prescribing physician based on the original indication (e.g., atrial fibrillation, mechanical valve) and bleeding risk. Many patients restart anticoagulation 1-4 weeks after stable hematoma resolution, sometimes with switch to a DOAC or referral for left atrial appendage closure.

faqs.question: What is decompressive craniectomy?
faqs.answer: Decompressive craniectomy is a procedure in which a large portion of the skull is removed (and not immediately replaced) and the dura widely opened to allow brain swelling. Used for refractory raised intracranial pressure after EDH or severe TBI. The skull is later replaced with cranioplasty at 3-6 months.

faqs.question: Are most epidural hematomas associated with skull fracture?
faqs.answer: Yes — approximately 75-80% of cranial EDH have an identifiable skull fracture, typically in the temporal or temporo-parietal region overlying the middle meningeal artery. The remaining 20% occur without fracture, particularly in younger patients with more pliable skulls.

faqs.question: Can an epidural hematoma come back?
faqs.answer: Recurrence is uncommon after complete surgical evacuation with dural tack-up. New EDH at a different site reflects new trauma rather than recurrence. Patients with persistent coagulopathy or anticoagulation have higher repeat-bleeding risk, particularly with further injuries.

faqs.question: What is the role of helmet use in preventing epidural hematoma?
faqs.answer: Helmets reduce the force transmitted to the skull during impact and substantially lower the risk of EDH (and other head injuries) in cycling, motorcycling, contact sports, skiing, skateboarding, and equestrian activity. Mandatory helmet laws are associated with significant population-level reductions in severe head injury.

faqs.question: Will I have permanent neurological problems after epidural hematoma?
faqs.answer: Most patients who reach surgery before fixed pupil dilation recover well — 70-80% achieve good functional outcomes. Some have persistent deficits including weakness, language difficulty, attention problems, mood changes, or post-concussive symptoms. Multidisciplinary rehabilitation supports recovery; substantial improvement continues over months to years.

faqs.question: What should I watch for after I leave the hospital?
faqs.answer: Report any new severe headache, vomiting, increasing drowsiness, new weakness, vision change, slurred speech, or seizure to the neurosurgical team or emergency services immediately. Follow up with scheduled imaging and clinic visits, complete the prescribed antiepileptic course, and follow activity and driving restrictions until cleared.
